items.question: Who needs a clear Generative AI taxonomy?
items.answer: Students, product managers, and engineers benefit when they can separate generative products from discriminative ML, route learning to what-is-genai and llms-fundamentals, and avoid treating Gen AI as unrelated to classical ML.

items.question: What defines Generative AI in this curriculum?
items.answer: Systems whose primary capability is producing new data samples—text, images, code, audio, or structured content—from learned generative models, rather than returning only a fixed label or score for a given input.

items.question: When is a deep learning model not a Gen AI product?
items.answer: When deployment uses only a discriminative head—classification, regression, ranking, or scoring—even if the backbone was pretrained with generative or self-supervised objectives.

items.question: Where does Gen AI sit relative to Deep Learning?
items.answer: Gen AI is a generative branch under machine learning, usually implemented with deep neural networks today; it describes the generation task and product, not a strict layer above deep learning.

items.question: Why link out instead of teaching full fundamentals here?
items.answer: This topic owns taxonomy placement in Stage 2 classical ML; model types, history, scaling, and LLM behavior are canonical in Gen AI Fundamentals topics to prevent duplicated, shallow coverage.

items.question: How do you decide if a system is Generative AI?
items.answer: Inspect the primary user-facing output: if the system synthesizes novel content by sampling from a learned model (continued text, new images, generated code), it is Gen AI; if it only predicts labels or scores, it is discriminative ML.

items.question: Generative AI vs discriminative machine learning—what differs at deployment?
items.answer: Discriminative deployment maps input to a constrained prediction (class, probability, score). Generative deployment runs a sampling process that constructs new outputs token-by-token, pixel-by-pixel, or step-by-step from a learned conditional distribution.

items.question: Generative AI vs Deep Learning—are they competing categories?
items.answer: No. Deep learning names the neural network stack and representation learning approach; Generative AI names the generative task and product. Modern Gen AI is largely deep learning plus sampling, often after self-supervised pretraining.
